S'I i\'11 OF CALII ORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES corvtMISSION 
1 to:!<} Strl'l't • Suitl• :iooo • SananH'nlo, CA 95811 
I •JI h 1 I.!.! , (,ho • I ,I\ ( 'l I h ) I .! .! o H H h 
Keith Collins 
Jones & Mayer 
Deputy Cit) Attorney 
City of Santa Fe Springs 
3777 N. Harbor Blvd 
Fullerton, CA 92835 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-184 
Dear Mr. Collins: 
September 5, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Santa Fe Springs 
Councilmembers Juanita A. Trujuillo, Jay Samo, William K. Rounds, and Richard J. Moore 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ''Act") 1 and 
Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, 
including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We arc also not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section l 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section l097. l (c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section l 097.1 (c)(4 ).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the·requestor." (See Section I 097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Are Councilmembers Trujuillo, Samo, Rounds, and Moore, disqualified by either the Act or 
Section I 090 from making a decision to enter into an agreement with a company to slurry-seal all 
residential neighborhood streets in the City? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Notwithstanding the fact that all Councilmembcrs live on a street that would be re­
sealed, they are not prohibited from taking part in the decisions in question under either the Act or 
Section I 090. The effect of a decision concerning repair or maintenance of the streets is not 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Go\'ernment Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Comm1ss10n are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


-· 
File No. A-17-184 
Page No. 2 
material under applicable conflict regulations, and with respect to Section I 090, the 
Councilmembers do not have financial interests in the contract at issue under these facts. 
FACTS 
You are the Deputy City Attorney for the City of Santa re Spring& and seek advice on 
behalf of Couneilmembers Trujuillo, Samo, Rounds, and Moore. The City Council would like to 
enler into an ag,rt:cment \\ ilh a company to Ǚlurry-st:al all residential ncighborhooJ sticels in the 
City. All four Councilmembers live on a street that would be re-sealed under this agreement. In 
your follow-up email dated August 18, 2017, you note that all Council members own the properties 
in question, and that a company has not yet been selected to perform the work. 
ANALYSIS 
Political Reform Act 
Section 87 l 00 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards 
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material 
depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are 
defined in Section 87103. 
Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the 
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. Based upon the facts provided, the only interests 
identified arc the Councilmembcrs' interests in their residences. Accordingly, we must detennine 
whether there is a foreseeable and material financial effect on these real property interests from 
decisions regarding lhe ro_ad slurry-seal project. 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if th!! interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 18701 (a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial 
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 1870l(b).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. 
(Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in 
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5. 
Based upon the facts provided, the Councilmembers' residences are not explicitly involved 
in the decisions in question and a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable only if the effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. For properties not 
explicitly involved in the decisions, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under 


File No. A-I 7-184 
Page No. 3 
which a reasonably foreseeable effect on the interests is material. As most pertinent to the facts 
provided, a foreseeable effect will be material if the decisions: 
"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, 
stonn drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an 
interest will receive new or impro\'ed serviceǚ that arc distinguishable from 
improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly 
l)itu.ile<l p10pl!rtit:s in thl! official's jut i!idiction or whcrl! the official will otherwise 
receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision; 
* * * 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property ... 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Despite the materiality standards set forth above or the fact that work involving the street 
slurry-seal will involve property within 500 feet of the Councilmembers' real property, Regulation 
l 8702.2(c)( 1) provides that a financial effect of a decision is not material if the decision "solely 
concerns repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or 
similar facilities." The decision to slurry-seal the City's streets concerns repairs or maintenance that 
fall within the exception of 18702.2(c)(l) because the decision described docs not appear to provide 
"new and improved services" but rather to maintain existing infrastructure. (Torres Advice Letter, 
A-17-061.} Therefore, the effect of the decision on the Councilmembers' property interests is not 
material. 
Section 1090 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People , .. Honig ( 1996} 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all 
parties. (Thomson v. Call ( I 985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one 
member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the 
interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the 
contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section I 090. These steps are: ( 1) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, ( 6) does the rule of necessity apply? 


File No. A-17-184 
Page No. 4 
In this case, the only step at issue is Step Four, does the official have a financial interest in 
the contract. Although Section 1090 does not specifically define "financial interest," the term has 
been liberally interpreted. (See People v. Deysher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146.) "The government's 
right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public officer is diminished as effectively where the 
officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as where he acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass 
(2001) IOI Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 (citations omitted).) 
The potential comraǛl you ha\ c idcnli!icd im olve::, the slun y-::ical resurfacing of all 
residential streets in the City. 
Nonetheless, an official has a financial interest in a contract only when there is a sufficient 
connection between the contract in question and interest held by the official. Under this formulation 
of"financial interest," it would be a clear Section 1090 conflict of interest if any of the 
Councilmembers had a financial relationship with, or interest in, a potential contractor to provide 
services under the agreement, or a financial relationship with a contracting entity. However, under 
these unique facts, the officials do not have a financial interest in the City's contract as 
contemplated by Section 1090 simply because the officials' properties, in addition to numerous 
other properties along all residential streets in the City, owned by a large and diverse portion of the 
public that the officials serve, will peripherally benefit from the contract.2 Based on the facts 
provided, we conclude that Section I 090 does not apply in this context. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
Q:y:2 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
1 Additionally, an official has a non-interest in the receipt of public services pro\'1ded by his or her agency or 
board as long as he or she receives them in the same manner as 1f he or she were not a public official. (Section I 091.5 
(a)(3).

Councilmembers do not have financial interests in the contract at issue under these facts.
